Enemy force including damaged battleship expected return Taranto on mean course 340° to 40°18'N, 17°03'N, thence 040°. If A/S measures force retirement, P 36 keep north of 40°15'N and Upholder and Proteus south of that latitude, former west and latter east of 17°05'E. (29) 23 Mar 1942 HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) fired four torpedoes against the Italian battleship Littorio in the Gulf of Taranto in position 40°11'N, 17°00'E. No hits were obtained. According to Italian sources Littorio was escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Bersagliere and Ascari and proceeding to Taranto. A torpedo track was observed crossing 200 meters ahead of the battleship. 1230 B/23 - Arrived in position 40°12'N, 17°03'E as ordered in Capt. S 10's signal timed 2339 B/22 to intercept the reported damaged battleship. 1720 B/23 - Heard heavy HE bearing 180° moving west fast. Closed at full speed. Visibility was very poor in the heavy rain and high sea. 1736 B/23 - In position 40°11'N, 17°00'E sighted a battleship [Littorio] steering 360° at 20 knots. 1738 B/23 - Fired four torpedoes from 4000 yards but the battleship zigged away after firing so all torpedoes missed. 2000 B/23 - Surfaced and passed a report to Malta. (26) 26 Mar 1942 Around 1645 B/26, HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) ended her 27th war patrol (25th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (26) 6 Apr 1942 Around 2000 B/6, HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) departed Malta for her 28th war patrol (26h in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to carry out a special operation (details to follow) and then to patrol off Tripoli, Libya. This was to be her last patrol before returning to the U.K. to refit. 9 Apr 1942 In the evening, Captain Robert Wilson, RM and Lance Corporal Charles Parker of the Beds and Herts Regiment landed two SIS agents by Folbots near Sousse, Tunisia. The two SIS agents (the Egyptian Victor Mantoura and the Syrian Georges de Dryère) were to enter Libya through Tunisia for intelligence activities but both were arrested by the Vichy police. Captain Wilson and Lance Corporal Parker returned to HMS Upholder after having landed the agents. HMS Upholder reported the operation completed in her signal timed 2301 B/9 addressed to Capt. S 10 and repeated to HMS Unbeaten. 11 Apr 1942 At 0340 B/11, in position 35°34'N, 12°17'E, Captain Wilson was transferred from HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) to HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, DSO, RN). HMS Unbeaten was on passage from Malta to Gibraltar. Wilson was the last man to see the crew of HMS Upholder. Lance Corporal Parker remained on board HMS Upholder and was lost with the submarine. On the two submarines parting company, as to her patrol orders, HMS Upholder was to patrol in ' area T ' to the south of 34°40'N and between 11°00'E and 12°50'E. The extreme south-east area of ' Area T ' seems the most interesting area and we presume Lt.Cdr. Wanklyn to have thought likewise. Also while proceeding to this area an important enemy convoy route (as given in Upholders patrol orders) could be followed as far a was possible. For some data regarding HMS Upholder's last war patrol plotted on a map see the map below; 11 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 2155 B/11, Capt. S.10 informed HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) that at 2100 B/11 a transport (motor vessel) and a destroyer had been sighted in position 33°19'N, 12°53'E, proceeding on course 345° at 15 knots. This must have referred to the Italian transport Gino Allegri (6836 GRT, built 1941) which had departed Tripoli around 1900 B/11 for Naples escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Centauro. It is to be expected that Lt.Cdr. Wanklyn would try to intercept this ship during the night of 11/12 April. No attacks were however reported by the Italians though it is always possible that one was made which was not observed. Having either made a failed attack on this convoy or having failed to intercept this convoy at all it seems likely that Lt.Cdr. Wanklyn would proceeded further to the south. It would be logical for him to proceed to the south-east part of area T as HMS Upholder would now be near the eastern edge anyway. Enemy convoys leaving Tripoli would most likely all proceed through the south-eastern part of area T before taking one of several possible convoy routes. Lt.Cdr. Wanklyn had also patrolled that area as recently as February 1942 and had succeeded in sinking an enemy transport back then. HMS Urge was en-route to patrol in the area between Pantelleria and Lampione Islands. An area where the reported convoy was expected through proceed through so HMS Urge continued her passage. While still en route to this area HMS Urge encountered a southbound convoy late in the morning of the 12th and attacked it. [See the page of HMS Urge for the details.] HMS Urge made no direct enemy report on this convoy for just after the attack a large modern transport was seen proceeding to the north-west. This must have been the Gino Allegri and her escort. [There is no mention of an escort in the patrol report of HMS Urge which never came closer then 5 to 6 nautical miles.] At 2250 B/12, HMS Urge finally cleared a signal to Capt. S.10 giving details of her exploits that day and also reporting the southbound convoy. Apparently no signal was sent to HMS Upholder regarding this southbound convoy so she must have been unaware of it. (31) 12 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 2252 B/12, the Capt. S.10 signalled the following orders to HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) and HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, RN). They were to take up the following positions by midnight during the night of 14/15 April. HMS Urge proceed through position 35°00'N, 13°10'E to position 'X' which was 33°11'N, 14°09'E. HMS Upholder proceed through position 33°25'N, 13°10'E to position 'Y' which was 33°10'N, 14°23'E. HMS Thrasher proceed through position 32°50'N, 16°00'E to position 'Y' which was 33°09'N, 14°37'E. Object of interception is an important convoy, expected mean line of advance 235° through position 'X' about dawn on the 15th. If forced to retire HMS Urge was to keep west of 14°15'E, HMS Thrasher east of 14°30'E and HMS Upholder between these longitudes. In a signal timed 1026 B/13, Capt. S.10 informed the submarines that the mean line of advance of the convoy would be 235° through position 'Y' about dawn on the 15th. Apparently there had been a mistake in the original signal. In a signal timed 2355 B/14, HMS Thasher was ordered to patrol in position Z - 335° - 5 nautical miles and HMS Urge in position X - 115° - 5 nautical miles. The important convoy referred to was en-route from Italy to Tripoli. It was made up of the transports Vettor Pisani (Italian, 6339 GRT, built 1939) and Reichenfels (German, 7744 GRT, built 1936). They had departed Naples around 0730 B/13 for Tripoli. They were escorted by the Italian destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Nicolo Zeno and the torpedo boat Pegaso. The convoy merged with two more convoys, the first convoy to join was made up of the transport Reginaldo Giuliani (Italian, 6837 GRT, built 1942) which had departed Brindisi around 1000 B/13, escorted by the Italian destroyer Mitragliere and the Italian torpedo boat Aretusa. On these three convoys merging Aretusa parted company. Later also a convoy made up of the transport Ravello (Italian, 6142 GRT, built 1941) joined. It had had departed Taranto around 1200 B/13 escorted by the Italian destroyers Freccia and Turbine. The combined convoy arrived at Tripoli around 0930 B/15. None of the submarines on the patrol line reported being in contact with the convoy. (31) 13 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1015 B/13, the Capt. S.10 signalled the following orders to HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN). Patrol between 33°08'N, 12°56'E and 33°10'N, 12°00'E for westbound traffic until necessary to comply with my 2252 B/12. It was expected that two enemy fast transports would leave Tripoli after having discharged their cargoes. Around 1900 B/13, indeed the transports Monreale (Italian, 5323 GRT, built 1941) and Unione ((Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1942) departed Tripoli for Naples escorted by the Italian destroyer Premuda and the Italian torpedo boat Lince. This convoy arrived safely at Naples around 1140 B/15 although the Lince had proceeded to Cagliari due to the heavy weather that was encountered. (31) 15 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1347 B/15, the Capt. S.10 signalled the following orders to HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN). At 2031 B/15, Upholder was to leave patrol and proceed through positions 33°25'N, 13°50'E, 33°25'N, 12°50'E, 34°35'N, 12°30'E and 35°24'N, 14°10'E. She was to arrive at Malta P.M. on the 18th. Plotting this route on the map it was obvious that between positions 33°25'N, 12°50'E and 34°35'N, 12°30'E, HMS Upholder was proceeding along an Italian convoy route. (31) 19 Apr 1942 Now being a day overdue, HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) was ordered by Capt S. 10 in his signal timed 2137 B/19, to report her position. No reply was received nor did HMS Upholder arrive at Malta. (31) What happened to HMS Upholder ? The only correct answer to this question is actually quite simple, unless the wreck is located nobody knows. HMS Upholder was last sighted at 0340 B/11, when in position 35°34'N, 12°17'E, Capt. Wilson was transferred to HMS Unbeaten. HMS Upholder was subsequently to patrol in ' Area T '. This area is quite large, as one see the map [see the event for 11 April 1942 for this map]. HMS Upholder could have gone anywhere within this area according to her orders. Now where in ' Area T ' would Lt.Cdr. Wanklyn thought he could have the best chance of gaining contact on the enemy and attack them ?. It leaves little doubt that Lt.Cdr. Wanklyn, which knew that he was to take HMS Upholder back to the U.K. after this patrol for a planned refit at the Chatham Dockyard, was keen to add some more enemy ships to his bag. According to us, the uboat.net crew, the most likely area to gain more successes would be the south-east corner of ' Area T ' which is the closest to Tripoli. This is also the area where Lt.Cdr. Wanklyn had his last success in this area as recently as February 1942. Also to get to the south-east part of ' Area T ' an important Italian convoy route could be covered. This is also the reason why Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Upholder to take cover this route as far as possible during her return to Malta on completion of her patrol. Once again you can see this quite clearly on the map. If Lt.Cdr. Wanklyn took HMS Upholder to the south-east part of ' Area T ', the Italian minefield ' S 1 ' [once again see the map] is placed more or less in the middle of this area. Other dangers in this area would be the frequent enemy A/S patrols but there are no reports of attacks on an enemy submarine in this area during the time HMS Upholder could have been there. Following her patrol in ' Area T ', HMS Upholder was ordered to ' Position Y ' on the patrol line which was further to be made up of HMS Urge and HMS Thrasher. Later, when it became obvious to Capt. S.10 that an enemy convoy was due to leave Tripoli for Italy via the western route and realising that HMS Upholder had some time at hand before she was to leave ' Area T ' for ' Position Y ' he ordered her to patrol on a line between positions 33°08'N, 12°56'E and 33°10'N, 12°00'E until it was necessary to leave for ' Position Y ' while passing through position 33°25'N, 13°10'E en-route [Once again for the location of this patrol line see the map]. Was HMS Upholder still active when these orders were received ?, and where was she ?. It is impossible to answer these questions. If HMS Upholder patrolled on the line between positions 33°08'N, 12°56'E and 33°10'N, 12°00'E she well may have encountered either an enemy convoy or enemy patrols when due to leave so as to be in time in ' Position Y ' she may have been delayed. Lt.Cdr. Wanklyn may therefore have decided to proceed directly from the patrol line to ' Position Y '. If one draws a line from the most eastern position of this patrol line, 33°08'N, 12°56'E, to ' Position Y ', one can see on the map clearly that that line would cross the northern ends of the Italian minefields ' F ', ' E ', ' D ' and ' C '. It is also to be noted that, at least, HMS Thrasher tried to make contact through SS/T, with HMS Upholder on the patrol line to the east of Tripoli. It was common for submarines on a patrol line to communicate under water to establish their relative positions to each other. No reply ever came from HMS Upholder which makes one think she most likely never made it to ' Position Y '. The position in which the Italian torpedo boat Pegaso is supposed to have attacked a submarine which in the past was thought to possibly have been HMS Upholder is also plotted on the map but given the orders sent to HMS Upholder it makes no sense at all for her to be anywhere near that position. According to the report of the Pegaso she actually did not drop any depth charges at all. It were German aircraft of the convoy's air escort that had dropped bombs in this position claiming that an oil patch had been seen following their attacks. We, the uboat.net crew, consider the most likely cause of the loss of HMS Upholder is that she was mined in the Tripoli area. Waters are not that deep there but, unfortunately, due to the Qadhafi-regime in the past and nowadays unstable political and security situation in Lybia, it is to this day, impossible to undertake (sonar) searches for her wreck. And, as said in the beginning, unless the wreck is found we do not know what happened to HMS Upholder. The torpedo boats Calliope and Perseo sailed from Tripoli to reinforce the convoy and joined it at 1150 B/23. The convoy arrived at Tripoli at 1525 B/23-1735 B/23. (26)
For the daily and attack positions of HMS Upholder during her 26th war patrol see the map below.

22 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1018 B/22, the Capt. S.10 informed all submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla which were at sea that at 0925 B/22 three merchant ships, two cruisers and ten destroyers had been sighted in position 35°18'N, 18°40'E proceeding on course 190° at speeds of 8 to 12 knots

In a signal timed 1111 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that eight merchant merchant ships, two battleships, five cruisers and fifteen destroyers had been sighted in position 35°12'N, 18°22'E on course 190° at a speed of 8 knots.

In a signal timed 1336 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that an enemy battleship of the Littorio-class, two cruisers of the Trento / Condottieri-class, four destroyers, three merchant ships and three enemy aircraft had been reported in position 34°48'N, 18°30'E proceeding on course 190° at a speed of 10 knots.

The following submarines were the ones that were on patrol at that time and thus must have received these signals; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN), HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN).

Presumably HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) also received these signals as she was also on patrol but she was to the north of Sicily at that time so given the position of the convoy sighted the signals were of no use to her. (28)

23 Feb 1942 At 0700 B/23, HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) dived 12 nautical miles north of her ordered patrol position. She had been delayed due to the weather conditions. Visibility was poor.

At 0810 B/23, a Cant. Z 506 B was seen patrolling in position 32°55'N, 14°48'E. No shipping was in sight but it was most likely that this seaplane was part of the air escort of the imported convoy that was expected.
At 0904 B/23, fast HE was picked up bearing 190° moving westwards. Nothing was in sight with a maximum visibility of 5000 yards. The HE moved to the northward and then to the eastward.
At 0957 B/23, a long series of depth charges started.
At 1000 B/23, HMS Upholder was finally in her ordered patrol position.
At 1034 B/23, HE was picked up moving eastward.

At 2145 B/23, Capt. S 10's signal 0530 B/30 was received. This ordered HMS Upholder to patrol in position 150° - 16 nautical miles from her original patrol position. This signal had not been received earlier as HMS Upholder had been dived and earlier that day, at 0800 B/23 only a part had been received due to bad atmospheric conditions. (26)

23 Feb 1942 In signals timed 1152 B/23 and 1203 B/23, Capt. S.10 sent the following orders; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) leave their patrol positions at 2000 B/23 through positions 35°00'N, 14°12'E, and 35°00'N, 14°18'E respectively to arrive off Marsamxett harbour at 0715 B/25.
HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'K', 34°20'N, 11°10'E - 000° - 40 - 60 through position 33°25'N, 13°45'E.
HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'G', 35°05'N, 12°05'E - 000° - 50 - 40 through positions 33°05'N, 14°11'E and 35°00'N, 13°05'E.

HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'L', south of 34°15'N and west of 11°40'E. She was to proceed through positions 33°00'N, 14°11'E and 33°50'N, 13°20'E.

HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'H', 35°15'N, 11°00'E - 000° - 40 - 50 proceeding through positions 33°00'N, 14°32'E and 35°00'N, 12°30'E.

HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'N', south of 33°45'N and between 11° 50'E and 12°55'E proceeding through positions 32°50'N, 14°45'E and 33.20'N, 13.45'E.

HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'J', 36°54'N, 15°24'E - 330° - 40 - 70 proceeding through positions 32°38'N, 15°00'E and 35°00'N, 15°30'E.
They were not to attack submarines encountered during the night of 23/24 February to prevent friendly fire incidents from happening. (28)

24 Feb 1942 At 2230 B/24, while north-west of Tripoli, in position 33°09'N, 12°47'E, HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) sighted two ships bearing 210°. Enemy course was 335° at 15 knots. HMS Upholder gave chase at full speed on course 280° and closed to 3000 yards.
At 2315 B/24, both ships were identified as two destroyers / torpedo boats. The chase was then broken off.
This were most likely the torpedo boats Circe and Pallade. They sailed from Tripoli at 1830 B/24-1906 B/24 for an A/S search and returned at 0938 B/26-0958 B/26. (26)

24 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1251 B/24, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN), HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) of the following; Area intelligence. Area G probable enemy route 35°55'N, 12°10'E to 35°05'N, 12°55'E. Small sweeper usually encountered between Lampedusa and Lampion and M.A.3. may operate from former. Areas L and N , enemy route number 7 buoy Kerkennah 33°51'N, 11°10'E - 33°06'N, 12°00'E. Coasters may anchor off Turgeuness and Zuara at night. (28)
25 Feb 1942 At 0900 B/24, while north-west of Tripoli, in position 32°55'N, 12°56'E, HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) sighted a small auxiliary brig steering westwards.
This vessel was seen returning towards Tripoli at 1210 B/24. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.] (26)

26 Feb 1942 At 0508 B/26, while west of Tripoli, in position 32°52'N, 12°58'E, HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) sighted an object to the westward and an attack was started. The target was thought to be a submarine at first but was soon seen to be two small destroyers / torpedo boats. It was noticed that from the bridges of these ships some cigarette ends were thrown overboard.
At 0512 B/26, HMS Upholder dived to avoid being sighted. The destroyers / torpedo boats appeared to be hunting.
At 0530 B/26, HMS Upholder surfaced when the enemy got clear but on surfacing found them laying stopped 1000 yards to the southward.
At 0531 B/26, HMS Upholder dived again and A/S transmissions were picked up but the enemy was not in contact.
At 0800 B/26, two Spica-class torpedo boats were observed to return to Tripoli from their patrol close inshore.
This were again most likely the torpedo boats Circe and Pallade. They sailed from Tripoli at 1830 B/24-1906 B/24 for an A/S search and returned at 0938 B/26-0958 B/26.
At 0930 B/26, the torpedo boat Generale Antonio Cantore was seen coming down the swept channel from the direction of Tripoli.
At 1405 B/26, the Generale Antonio Cantore was seen to return to Tripoli at 20 knots.

According to Italian records the Generale Antonio Cantore sailed from Tripoli at 1150 B/26, escorting out four German F-lighters, she returned at 0910 B/27. This makes it unlikely that it was her unless there is an error somewhere in the times recorded. She certainly was in the area though. (26)

27 Feb 1942 HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian transport Tembien (5584 GRT, built 1914) about 25 nautical miles west of Tripoli, Libya in position 32°55'N, 12°42'E. Tembien was escorted by the Italian destroyer Strale. The destroyer hunted the submarine dropping nine depth charges before starting to rescue the survivors. She was later joined in her efforts by the auxiliary Laurana and the torpedo boats Clio and Generale Antonio Cantore. Tembien carried 498 PoW, 137 Italians and 20 Germans. Strale picked up 155 survivors (68 Italians, 9 Germans and 78 PoWs). The torpedo boat Clio found two survivors (1 Italian and 1 PoW), making the total number of survivors 157.
0900 B/27 - Two minesweepers were seen in the searched channel coming from the direction of Tripoli. They were later seen to return to Tripoli. They were seen to be a tug and an auxiliary brig.
1845 B/27 - In position 32°55'N, 12°46'E, heard faint HE bearing 060° and sighted a large merchant vessel and one destroyer / torpedo boat in the haze. Enemy course was 280°. Started attack.
1905 B/27 - In position 32°56'N, 12°43'E fired three torpedoes at the merchant vessel that was of about 5500 tons, half passenger type and she was dazzle painted.
1907 B/27 - Two torpedo hits were obtained. The escort shortly increased speed and started depth charging but stopped after dropping eight depth charges to pick up survivors.
1925 B/27 - Heard the ship breaking up.
1950 B/27 - The escort moved off towards Tripoli. (26)

1 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 1203 B/1, the Capt. S.10 sent the following instructions on leaving patrol to the submarines mentioned; HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) was to arrive at Malta on 4 March and was to proceed through positions 35°40'N, 12°00'E and 35°40'N, 14°20'E on the way back. HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) was to arrive at Malta on 5 March and was to proceed through positions 34°00'N, 12°50'E and 35°32'N, 14°14'E on the way back. HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) was to arrive at Malta on 5 March and was to proceed through positions 37°02'N, 15°40'E and 35°51'N, 15°15'E on the way back. (29)
3 Mar 1942 Around 1900 B/3, HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) left patrol to return to Malta. (30)
5 Mar 1942 Around 0650 B/5, HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) ended her 26th war patrol (24th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (26)
14 Mar 1942 Around 1930 B/14, HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) departed Malta for her 27th war patrol (25th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the Gulf of Taranto.

At 2215 B/14, east-north-east of Malta, in position 36°01'N, 14°56'E, a motor torpedo boat was seen proceeding southwards at high speed. HMS Upholder dived until the HE disappeared. It was thought this may have been XMAS (a captured Italian MAS boat which was used mainly for special operations and air/sea rescue duties out of Malta) which was known to be at sea. [No further details available.]

16 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 2039 B/16, the Capt. S.10 send the following orders to HMS Proteus (Lt.Cdr. P.S. Francis, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) and HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN); Concentration off Taranto postponed. Proceed as follows: Upholder through position 39°35'N, 18°34'E and 40°00'N, 18°55'E to patrol west of a line 350° through the last position and south of 04°50'N. P 36 patrol within 30 miles Colonne south of 39°15'N. Proteus through position 37°30'N, 20°30'E to patrol within 30 miles of cape Dukato west of 20°45'E concentrating on the Levkas Channel. A full bow salvo must be retained for use after arrival off Taranto probably P.M. on 20 March. (26)

17 Mar 1942 At 1245 B/17, south of the Strait of Otranto, in position 39°52'N, 18°58'E, HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) sighted two destroyers proceeding southwards on a course of 190° at 25 knots.

We have been unable to identify these destroyers. The destroyer Saetta was escorting a convoy from Brindisi (departed at 0200 B/16) to Navarino (arrived at 1956 B/17), but she would not make 25 knots during this duty. (26)
18 Mar 1942 HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian submarine Tricheco east of Brindisi in the southern Adriatic in position 40°42'N, 17°57'E.

0830 B/18 - In position 40°42'N, 18°00'E, sighted two auxiliary minesweepers proceeding on a course of 110°. They passed Brindisi and then continued on course 120°. [We have been unable to identify these vessels.]
1407 B/18 - In position 40°43'N, 17°59'E, a sloop was seen steering southwards on a course of 185°. [We have been unable to identify this vessel.]

1457 B/18 - In position 40°42'N, 17°57'E, sighted a small merchant vessel and one escort steering 110° at 7 knots. Started attack. The merchant vessel entered Brindisi harbour at 1520 B/18 having zigged away twice and the attack was broken off. She passed at a range of 3500 yards. The passenger vessel Eolo (704 GRT, built 1936) entered Brindisi at 1430 B/18, but there is no mention of an escort vessel in Italian records. Also the time does no match.

1535 B/18 - In position 40°41'N, 17°57'E sighted a Perla class submarine steering for Brindisi and attempted to attack but could not close to less than 5000 yards. This submarine was Ondina (T.V. Gabriele Andolfi) returning to Brindisi after morning exercises.
1708 B/18 - In position 40°45'N, 17°57'E sighted another submarine steering 185° at 12 knots. Started attack.

1726 B/18 - Fired four torpedoes from 550 yards. Two hits were obtained and the submarine quickly sank. [According to Italian sources the submarine was under Capitano di Corvetta Giovanni Cunsolo and had sailed from Augusta for Brindisi. Four officers (including Cunsolo) and seven ratings were picked up by the transport Nazario Sauro and the sailing vessel S. Spiridione. One officer and 37 ratings were lost.]
1830 B/18 - Several small vessels were seen searching the attack area.
2045 B/18 - Surfaced and ran to the eastwards after reloading.
2205 B/18 - Encountered a motor torpedo boat patrol. HMS Upholder dived to run clear but they remained in close company for about an hour. [We have been unable to identify these vessels.] (26)

18 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 1411 B/18, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Proteus (Lt.Cdr. P.S. Francis, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) and HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN); P 36, leave position 39°05'N, 17°38'E about 2000 B/19 to arrive in position 'A' (40°08'N, 17°08'E) at dawn/20. Proteus, leave area A.M./19 through position 39°30'N, 17°45'E about 0400 B/20 to arrive in position 'B' (40°00'N, 17°17'E) at noon/20. Upholder, leave as necessary by reverse of northward route and through position 39°30'N, 18°00'E about 0500 B/20 to arrive in position 'C' (39°50'N, 17°21'E) at dusk/20. Position 'D' will not be occupied. (29)
19 Mar 1942 HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) sank the Italian auxiliary minesweeper B.14/Maria (22 GRT) with gunfire, about 10 nautical miles north of Otranto, Italy.
0737 B/19 - In position 40°18'N, 18°28'E sighted four small craft which turned out to be a diesel trawler and three fishing smacks. Closed for gun action.

0843 B/19 - Surfaced 100 yards from the trawler and gestured the crew to abandon ship. Seven Rounds were fired from 50 yards for six hits. The vessel caught fire and settled having three large holes on the waterline.
0858 B/19 - Dived and retired to the east. (26)

20 Mar 1942 At 1700 B/20, HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) arrived in the Gulf of Taranto in position 'C' (39°50'N, 17°21'E) as had been ordered in Capt. S 10's signal timed 1411 B/18. (26)
22 Mar 1942 At 0215 B/22, HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) heard the HE of a motor torpedo boat passing from the north to the east. Nothing could be seen though.
At 0420 B/22, the motor torpedo boat returned to the northward. At the same time HE of a destroyer / torpedo boat (280 revs) proceeded eastwards.

The destroyer Geniere sailed from Taranto at 0158 B/22 to participate in the Second Battle of Sirte. The destroyer Scirocco sailed from Taranto at 0250 B/22 to participate in the Second Battle of Sirte, the bad weather forced her to turn back, and she foundered at 0545 B/23. HMS Upholder must have heard one of them.
At 0650 B/22, the HE of a motor torpedo boat was picked up moving northwards. Again nothing could be seen.
There is no mention of any MAS boats operating from Taranto on this day (probably due to the bad weather).

A convoy consisting of the transport Enrichetta (Italian, 4652 GRT, built 1907) and the tanker Ardor (Italian, 8960 GRT, built 1927), escorted by the destroyer Euro and the torpedo boat Aretusa, sailed from Taranto at 0800 B/22-0918 B/22 for Brindisi and Bari. Enrichetta and Aretusa arrived at Brindisi at 1430 B/23, Ardor and Euro, reinforced by the destroyer Sebenico, arrived at Bari at 1915 B/23. Apparently HMS Upholder did not detect this convoy. (26)

22 Mar 1942 In a signal timed 2339 B/22, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Proteus (Lt.Cdr. P.S. Francis, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) and HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN); Establish patrol as follows by noon/23 P 36 in position 'R', 40°19'N, 17°06'E. Upholder in position 'S', 40°12'N, 17°03'E. Proteus in position 'T', 40°08'N, 17°08'E. Enemy force including damaged battleship expected return Taranto on mean course 340° to 40°18'N, 17°03'N, thence 040°. If A/S measures force retirement, P 36 keep north of 40°15'N and Upholder and Proteus south of that latitude, former west and latter east of 17°05'E. (29)

23 Mar 1942 HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) fired four torpedoes against the Italian battleship Littorio in the Gulf of Taranto in position 40°11'N, 17°00'E. No hits were obtained. According to Italian sources Littorio was escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Bersagliere and Ascari and proceeding to Taranto. A torpedo track was observed crossing 200 meters ahead of the battleship.
1230 B/23 - Arrived in position 40°12'N, 17°03'E as ordered in Capt. S 10's signal timed 2339 B/22 to intercept the reported damaged battleship.
1720 B/23 - Heard heavy HE bearing 180° moving west fast. Closed at full speed. Visibility was very poor in the heavy rain and high sea.
1736 B/23 - In position 40°11'N, 17°00'E sighted a battleship [Littorio] steering 360° at 20 knots.
1738 B/23 - Fired four torpedoes from 4000 yards but the battleship zigged away after firing so all torpedoes missed.
2000 B/23 - Surfaced and passed a report to Malta. (26)
26 Mar 1942 Around 1645 B/26, HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) ended her 27th war patrol (25th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (26)

6 Apr 1942 Around 2000 B/6, HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) departed Malta for her 28th war patrol (26h in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to carry out a special operation (details to follow) and then to patrol off Tripoli, Libya.
This was to be her last patrol before returning to the U.K. to refit.

9 Apr 1942 In the evening, Captain Robert Wilson, RM and Lance Corporal Charles Parker of the Beds and Herts Regiment landed two SIS agents by Folbots near Sousse, Tunisia. The two SIS agents (the Egyptian Victor Mantoura and the Syrian Georges de Dryère) were to enter Libya through Tunisia for intelligence activities but both were arrested by the Vichy police. Captain Wilson and Lance Corporal Parker returned to HMS Upholder after having landed the agents.
HMS Upholder reported the operation completed in her signal timed 2301 B/9 addressed to Capt. S 10 and repeated to HMS Unbeaten.

11 Apr 1942 At 0340 B/11, in position 35°34'N, 12°17'E, Captain Wilson was transferred from HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) to HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, DSO, RN). HMS Unbeaten was on passage from Malta to Gibraltar. Wilson was the last man to see the crew of HMS Upholder. Lance Corporal Parker remained on board HMS Upholder and was lost with the submarine.
On the two submarines parting company, as to her patrol orders, HMS Upholder was to patrol in ' area T ' to the south of 34°40'N and between 11°00'E and 12°50'E.

The extreme south-east area of ' Area T ' seems the most interesting area and we presume Lt.Cdr. Wanklyn to have thought likewise. Also while proceeding to this area an important enemy convoy route (as given in Upholders patrol orders) could be followed as far a was possible.
For some data regarding HMS Upholder's last war patrol plotted on a map see the map below;

11 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 2155 B/11, Capt. S.10 informed HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) that at 2100 B/11 a transport (motor vessel) and a destroyer had been sighted in position 33°19'N, 12°53'E, proceeding on course 345° at 15 knots.
This must have referred to the Italian transport Gino Allegri (6836 GRT, built 1941) which had departed Tripoli around 1900 B/11 for Naples escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Centauro.

It is to be expected that Lt.Cdr. Wanklyn would try to intercept this ship during the night of 11/12 April. No attacks were however reported by the Italians though it is always possible that one was made which was not observed.

Having either made a failed attack on this convoy or having failed to intercept this convoy at all it seems likely that Lt.Cdr. Wanklyn would proceeded further to the south. It would be logical for him to proceed to the south-east part of area T as HMS Upholder would now be near the eastern edge anyway. Enemy convoys leaving Tripoli would most likely all proceed through the south-eastern part of area T before taking one of several possible convoy routes. Lt.Cdr. Wanklyn had also patrolled that area as recently as February 1942 and had succeeded in sinking an enemy transport back then.

HMS Urge was en-route to patrol in the area between Pantelleria and Lampione Islands. An area where the reported convoy was expected through proceed through so HMS Urge continued her passage. While still en route to this area HMS Urge encountered a southbound convoy late in the morning of the 12th and attacked it. [See the page of HMS Urge for the details.] HMS Urge made no direct enemy report on this convoy for just after the attack a large modern transport was seen proceeding to the north-west. This must have been the Gino Allegri and her escort. [There is no mention of an escort in the patrol report of HMS Urge which never came closer then 5 to 6 nautical miles.] At 2250 B/12, HMS Urge finally cleared a signal to Capt. S.10 giving details of her exploits that day and also reporting the southbound convoy.
Apparently no signal was sent to HMS Upholder regarding this southbound convoy so she must have been unaware of it. (31)

12 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 2252 B/12, the Capt. S.10 signalled the following orders to HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) and HMS Thrasher (Lt. H.S. Mackenzie, RN).
They were to take up the following positions by midnight during the night of 14/15 April.
HMS Urge proceed through position 35°00'N, 13°10'E to position 'X' which was 33°11'N, 14°09'E.
HMS Upholder proceed through position 33°25'N, 13°10'E to position 'Y' which was 33°10'N, 14°23'E.
HMS Thrasher proceed through position 32°50'N, 16°00'E to position 'Y' which was 33°09'N, 14°37'E.

Object of interception is an important convoy, expected mean line of advance 235° through position 'X' about dawn on the 15th. If forced to retire HMS Urge was to keep west of 14°15'E, HMS Thrasher east of 14°30'E and HMS Upholder between these longitudes.

In a signal timed 1026 B/13, Capt. S.10 informed the submarines that the mean line of advance of the convoy would be 235° through position 'Y' about dawn on the 15th. Apparently there had been a mistake in the original signal.
In a signal timed 2355 B/14, HMS Thasher was ordered to patrol in position Z - 335° - 5 nautical miles and HMS Urge in position X - 115° - 5 nautical miles.

The important convoy referred to was en-route from Italy to Tripoli. It was made up of the transports Vettor Pisani (Italian, 6339 GRT, built 1939) and Reichenfels (German, 7744 GRT, built 1936). They had departed Naples around 0730 B/13 for Tripoli. They were escorted by the Italian destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Nicolo Zeno and the torpedo boat Pegaso.

The convoy merged with two more convoys, the first convoy to join was made up of the transport Reginaldo Giuliani (Italian, 6837 GRT, built 1942) which had departed Brindisi around 1000 B/13, escorted by the Italian destroyer Mitragliere and the Italian torpedo boat Aretusa. On these three convoys merging Aretusa parted company.
Later also a convoy made up of the transport Ravello (Italian, 6142 GRT, built 1941) joined. It had had departed Taranto around 1200 B/13 escorted by the Italian destroyers Freccia and Turbine.
The combined convoy arrived at Tripoli around 0930 B/15.
None of the submarines on the patrol line reported being in contact with the convoy. (31)
13 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1015 B/13, the Capt. S.10 signalled the following orders to HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN).
Patrol between 33°08'N, 12°56'E and 33°10'N, 12°00'E for westbound traffic until necessary to comply with my 2252 B/12.

It was expected that two enemy fast transports would leave Tripoli after having discharged their cargoes. Around 1900 B/13, indeed the transports Monreale (Italian, 5323 GRT, built 1941) and Unione ((Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1942) departed Tripoli for Naples escorted by the Italian destroyer Premuda and the Italian torpedo boat Lince. This convoy arrived safely at Naples around 1140 B/15 although the Lince had proceeded to Cagliari due to the heavy weather that was encountered. (31)
15 Apr 1942 In a signal timed 1347 B/15, the Capt. S.10 signalled the following orders to HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN).
At 2031 B/15, Upholder was to leave patrol and proceed through positions 33°25'N, 13°50'E, 33°25'N, 12°50'E, 34°35'N, 12°30'E and 35°24'N, 14°10'E. She was to arrive at Malta P.M. on the 18th.
Plotting this route on the map it was obvious that between positions 33°25'N, 12°50'E and 34°35'N, 12°30'E, HMS Upholder was proceeding along an Italian convoy route. (31)
19 Apr 1942 Now being a day overdue, HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) was ordered by Capt S. 10 in his signal timed 2137 B/19, to report her position.
No reply was received nor did HMS Upholder arrive at Malta. (31)
What happened to HMS Upholder ?
The only correct answer to this question is actually quite simple, unless the wreck is located nobody knows.
HMS Upholder was last sighted at 0340 B/11, when in position 35°34'N, 12°17'E, Capt. Wilson was transferred to HMS Unbeaten.

HMS Upholder was subsequently to patrol in ' Area T '. This area is quite large, as one see the map [see the event for 11 April 1942 for this map]. HMS Upholder could have gone anywhere within this area according to her orders.

Now where in ' Area T ' would Lt.Cdr. Wanklyn thought he could have the best chance of gaining contact on the enemy and attack them ?. It leaves little doubt that Lt.Cdr. Wanklyn, which knew that he was to take HMS Upholder back to the U.K. after this patrol for a planned refit at the Chatham Dockyard, was keen to add some more enemy ships to his bag.

According to us, the uboat.net crew, the most likely area to gain more successes would be the south-east corner of ' Area T ' which is the closest to Tripoli. This is also the area where Lt.Cdr. Wanklyn had his last success in this area as recently as February 1942. Also to get to the south-east part of ' Area T ' an important Italian convoy route could be covered. This is also the reason why Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Upholder to take cover this route as far as possible during her return to Malta on completion of her patrol. Once again you can see this quite clearly on the map.

If Lt.Cdr. Wanklyn took HMS Upholder to the south-east part of ' Area T ', the Italian minefield ' S 1 ' [once again see the map] is placed more or less in the middle of this area. Other dangers in this area would be the frequent enemy A/S patrols but there are no reports of attacks on an enemy submarine in this area during the time HMS Upholder could have been there.

Following her patrol in ' Area T ', HMS Upholder was ordered to ' Position Y ' on the patrol line which was further to be made up of HMS Urge and HMS Thrasher. Later, when it became obvious to Capt. S.10 that an enemy convoy was due to leave Tripoli for Italy via the western route and realising that HMS Upholder had some time at hand before she was to leave ' Area T ' for ' Position Y ' he ordered her to patrol on a line between positions 33°08'N, 12°56'E and 33°10'N, 12°00'E until it was necessary to leave for ' Position Y ' while passing through position 33°25'N, 13°10'E en-route [Once again for the location of this patrol line see the map].
Was HMS Upholder still active when these orders were received ?, and where was she ?. It is impossible to answer these questions.

If HMS Upholder patrolled on the line between positions 33°08'N, 12°56'E and 33°10'N, 12°00'E she well may have encountered either an enemy convoy or enemy patrols when due to leave so as to be in time in ' Position Y ' she may have been delayed. Lt.Cdr. Wanklyn may therefore have decided to proceed directly from the patrol line to ' Position Y '. If one draws a line from the most eastern position of this patrol line, 33°08'N, 12°56'E, to ' Position Y ', one can see on the map clearly that that line would cross the northern ends of the Italian minefields ' F ', ' E ', ' D ' and ' C '.

It is also to be noted that, at least, HMS Thrasher tried to make contact through SS/T, with HMS Upholder on the patrol line to the east of Tripoli. It was common for submarines on a patrol line to communicate under water to establish their relative positions to each other. No reply ever came from HMS Upholder which makes one think she most likely never made it to ' Position Y '.

The position in which the Italian torpedo boat Pegaso is supposed to have attacked a submarine which in the past was thought to possibly have been HMS Upholder is also plotted on the map but given the orders sent to HMS Upholder it makes no sense at all for her to be anywhere near that position. According to the report of the Pegaso she actually did not drop any depth charges at all. It were German aircraft of the convoy's air escort that had dropped bombs in this position claiming that an oil patch had been seen following their attacks.
We, the uboat.net crew, consider the most likely cause of the loss of HMS Upholder is that she was mined in the Tripoli area.

Waters are not that deep there but, unfortunately, due to the Qadhafi-regime in the past and nowadays unstable political and security situation in Lybia, it is to this day, impossible to undertake (sonar) searches for her wreck. And, as said in the beginning, unless the wreck is found we do not know what happened to HMS Upholder.
